column1: # Principle: Integer vs Decimal literal discrimination.
# AQL has TWO distinct numeric scalar types under the same Number root:
#   Scalar/Number/Integer  — written as digits with optional leading minus
#   Scalar/Number/Decimal  — written with a decimal point
# Both are subtypes of Number (which is itself a subtype of Scalar,
# which is a subtype of Any). This file pins down the literal-form
# rules and the type-tagging rule that follows from them. The chosen
# tag is what the engine's matchSignature inspects when picking an
# overload, so getting the literal recognisable correctly is a
# precondition for typed dispatch to work.
# Rules being asserted
# 1. A bare digit-only token is an Integer. (`42`, `0`, `-7`)
# 2. A token containing a `.` is a Decimal. (`3.14`, `-0.5`, `2.0`)
# 3. Both round-trip through the engine unchanged when there is no
#    word to consume them — the runner's renderer prints them in the
#    same form the tokenizer accepted.
# 4. The integer-only words `add`, `sub`, `mul` reject decimals at
#    sig-match time with a signature_error. They are not auto-coerced,
#    even when the value would be representable.
# Why this matters
# The integer-vs-decimal split is a foundational lattice decision: it
# means a 3-arg `add` overload could later be added on Decimal and a
# call site `1.0 add 2.0` would dispatch to Decimal-add. Without the
# split (or with auto-coercion), all numeric overloads would collapse
# into one, defeating the type-directed dispatch story.
# Spec runner notes
# The spec runner tokenises with strconv.ParseInt first, then
# strconv.ParseFloat — so any token Go's int64 parser accepts is an
# Integer; any token its float parser accepts (but the int parser
# does not) is a Decimal. The renderer round-trips with FormatInt
# for integers and FormatFloat with -1 precision for decimals (which
# preserves the input exactly for typical literal values).
# tokens

column3: formatDecimal preserves the decimal point — renders as "0.0", not "0", to keep Decimal visually distinct from Integer
whole-valued decimal renders as "2.0" (formatDecimal appends ".0" when there's no fractional part or exponent)
integer and decimal coexist; the type tag is now visible in the rendered form
add expects two integers; decimals fail the sig

column1: add 2 3.5
column3: mixed integer + decimal still rejects (no coercion)

column1: tag 3.14
column3: tag has no Decimal overload, so Decimal hits the [Any] sig

column1: tag -0.5
